---
title: Why BDM
subtitle: The problem with behavioral data, and what a shared framework buys us
---

## The problem

Every lab organizes behavioral data its own way. The same task produces
differently named files, differently shaped tables, and differently defined
variables in every lab that runs it — sometimes in every study. The result:
datasets that cannot be combined, tools that cannot be reused, and analyses
that start from scratch every time.[^search]

[^search]: Finding usable datasets is measurably hard; see Hulsebos et al.
(2024), [*It took longer than I was expecting*](https://doi.org/10.1145/3665939.3665959).

None of this is negligence. Behavioral data is genuinely harder to
standardize than it looks: a key press means nothing by itself — its meaning
depends on the task, the stimulus, the instructions, and the person. A format
for behavioral data has to carry that context. BDM is designed to.

## What a shared framework buys

- **Efficiency** — less bespoke work; code and tools transfer between studies.
- **Robustness** — fewer errors from ambiguous, idiosyncratic formats.
- **Transparency** — fewer hidden choices buried in analysis scripts.
- **Quality** — consistency and completeness can be checked automatically.
- **Usability** — datasets ship documented and ready to analyze.
- **Reuse and integration** — results can be replicated, extended, and merged
  across studies; large shared datasets become possible.

## Where BDM comes from

BDM builds on standards that earned their place: [tidy
data](https://www.jstatsoft.org/article/view/v059i10) for table shape,
[BIDS](https://bids.neuroimaging.io/) for dataset organization,
[xAPI](https://xapi.com/) for events, [schema.org](https://schema.org) and
[CSVW](https://www.w3.org/TR/tabular-data-primer/) for metadata. Where good
prior art exists, BDM adopts it; it only invents where cognitive science has
needs nothing else covers.

The framework is developed openly — see the [ecosystem](ecosystem.qmd), the
[roadmap](roadmap.qmd), and the [discussions on
GitHub](https://github.com/orgs/behaverse/discussions).

## The paper

The thinking behind BDM is laid out in [*The structure of behavioral
data*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.12583). If you use BDM in your research,
cite:

```bibtex
@misc{defossez2020structurebehavioraldata,
      title={The structure of behavioral data},
      author={Aurélien Defossez and Morteza Ansarinia and Brice Clocher and
              Emmanuel Schmück and Paul Schrater and Pedro Cardoso-Leite},
      year={2020},
      eprint={2012.12583},
      archivePrefix={arXiv},
      primaryClass={q-bio.NC},
      url={https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.12583},
}
```
